criterion_breakdown.label: Legal truth separation
criterion_breakdown.note: Best match for the truth-separation contract because projections stay disposable and subordinate to durable truth surfaces.

criterion_breakdown.label: Staff versus customer-safe visibility control
criterion_breakdown.note: Customer-safe redaction happens at projection build time, before any browser or native serialization boundary.

criterion_breakdown.label: Same-object and same-shell continuity
criterion_breakdown.note: Route-level envelopes can carry the exact shell, focus, and recovery anchors needed to preserve same-object continuity.

criterion_breakdown.label: Browser/native parity without duplicate business logic
criterion_breakdown.note: Typed projections let web and native share the same business semantics while differing only in embodiment.

criterion_breakdown.label: Stream delta and reconnect fitness
criterion_breakdown.note: Monotonic deltas, explicit cursors, and rebase contracts align directly with the named northbound and collaboration stream rules.

criterion_breakdown.label: Stale-view protection and rebase clarity
criterion_breakdown.note: The server can publish guard families, latest values, and typed rebase posture instead of leaving clients to infer them.

criterion_breakdown.label: Rebuildability from durable truth
criterion_breakdown.note: All caches and deltas remain disposable because route projections are rebuilt from durable truth rather than acting as primary state.

criterion_breakdown.label: Schema evolution and reader-window safety
criterion_breakdown.note: Versioned route envelopes give web, native, and tests one stable reader window for additive change.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance and cache invalidation complexity
criterion_breakdown.note: More server read models exist, but invalidation stays explicit and local to named surfaces rather than exploding client-side.

criterion_breakdown.label: Testing determinism and fixture friendliness
criterion_breakdown.note: Dedicated read models, stable selectors, and typed deltas are easier to fixture and regression-test consistently.

criterion_breakdown.label: Operational simplicity and debuggability
criterion_breakdown.note: Projection ownership is explicit per surface family, making incident analysis clearer than multi-client composition rules.

alternatives.label: Server-authored per-surface typed read models and deltas
risks: Requires explicit projector ownership and more schema discipline than a thin pass-through API.
Needs clear versioning and invalidation rules so projection sprawl does not become accidental duplication.
strengths: Best fit for the corpus's named route-level projections such as calm-shell frames, collaboration snapshots/deltas, portal workspaces, and governance snapshots.
Preserves customer-safe boundaries and stale/recovery posture as server-authored contracts instead of client heuristics.
Lets browser and native clients share one doctrine while keeping caches disposable.
alternatives.summary: The server owns route-level read models, stream deltas, stale guards, customer-safe redaction, and native/browser parity; clients consume and render those contracts rather than inventing legal posture from fragments.

criterion_breakdown.label: Legal truth separation
criterion_breakdown.note: Truth separation can be preserved in principle, but route meaning is more likely to drift once clients compose posture from fragments.

criterion_breakdown.label: Staff versus customer-safe visibility control
criterion_breakdown.note: Customer-safe boundaries become harder to enforce because clients must know which granular fields are forbidden to combine.

criterion_breakdown.label: Same-object and same-shell continuity
criterion_breakdown.note: Continuity can be approximated, but route and shell identity depend on each client recomposing equivalent anchors.

criterion_breakdown.label: Browser/native parity without duplicate business logic
criterion_breakdown.note: Web and native inevitably duplicate more composition and recovery logic when the server does less shaping.

criterion_breakdown.label: Stream delta and reconnect fitness
criterion_breakdown.note: Granular APIs can stream, but reconnect semantics become harder once the client owns composition of many moving pieces.

criterion_breakdown.label: Stale-view protection and rebase clarity
criterion_breakdown.note: Clients need to combine many stale bases and can more easily guess at rebase causes or route fallbacks.

criterion_breakdown.label: Rebuildability from durable truth
criterion_breakdown.note: Durable truth still exists, but recreating the exact client-composed state requires reproducing more hidden client rules.

criterion_breakdown.label: Schema evolution and reader-window safety
criterion_breakdown.note: Granular APIs can evolve independently, but the overall reader window is harder to reason about because view-model meaning is emergent.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance and cache invalidation complexity
criterion_breakdown.note: This option can reduce some server projection work and allow narrower fetches, which is its strongest tradeoff.

criterion_breakdown.label: Testing determinism and fixture friendliness
criterion_breakdown.note: Unit tests can cover some client composition logic, but end-to-end determinism suffers once many fragments must align at runtime.

criterion_breakdown.label: Operational simplicity and debuggability
criterion_breakdown.note: Short-term service topology is simpler, though runtime reasoning becomes split across multiple clients and caches.

alternatives.label: Thin backend plus client-composed view models from granular APIs
risks: Pushes stale-view logic, visibility fencing, and route continuity semantics into browser and native code.
Raises the odds that different clients compose different meanings from the same fragments.
strengths: Looks simpler initially because fewer dedicated server projections need to be maintained.
Can reduce some duplicated server read-side code for small or highly local screens.
alternatives.summary: Expose many smaller APIs and let browser/native clients assemble their own view models, status posture, and route state from granular domain fragments.

criterion_breakdown.label: Legal truth separation
criterion_breakdown.note: A mega-model exposes too much adjacent state to the client and makes it easier to treat hidden graph context as user-facing truth.

criterion_breakdown.label: Staff versus customer-safe visibility control
criterion_breakdown.note: Customer-safe and staff-only lanes are hardest to police when one large payload contains broad mixed visibility state.

criterion_breakdown.label: Same-object and same-shell continuity
criterion_breakdown.note: Shell and route ownership blur when the client can pivot locally through a broad graph without explicit route contracts.

criterion_breakdown.label: Browser/native parity without duplicate business logic
criterion_breakdown.note: Both clients still need substantial local selection and derivation logic, so parity remains fragile.

criterion_breakdown.label: Stream delta and reconnect fitness
criterion_breakdown.note: A large graph can support streams, but fine-grained invalidation and rebase semantics become much harder to type and explain.

criterion_breakdown.label: Stale-view protection and rebase clarity
criterion_breakdown.note: Clients see broad state drift without clear route-level stale guard families, making rebase posture murkier.

criterion_breakdown.label: Rebuildability from durable truth
criterion_breakdown.note: The client-side graph often accumulates hidden composition rules that are difficult to replay or rebuild deterministically.

criterion_breakdown.label: Schema evolution and reader-window safety
criterion_breakdown.note: A single mega-envelope increases blast radius for schema drift and widens the reader window problem.

criterion_breakdown.label: Performance and cache invalidation complexity
criterion_breakdown.note: Broad payloads and cross-cutting invalidation can quickly become expensive and opaque.

criterion_breakdown.label: Testing determinism and fixture friendliness
criterion_breakdown.note: Tests have to stand up a large shape and still reproduce local selection logic reliably.

criterion_breakdown.label: Operational simplicity and debuggability
criterion_breakdown.note: The runtime can appear centralized, but debugging which graph fragment produced which user-facing posture becomes difficult.

alternatives.label: Large unified graph or mega-workspace model with client-heavy selection and composition
risks: Blurs route ownership, visibility lanes, and customer-safe boundaries because the client sees too much at once.
Makes stale-view, rebase, and cache invalidation more implicit and harder to debug.
strengths: Promises fewer explicit route-specific envelopes and can simplify some bulk-fetch patterns.
May look attractive for exploratory consoles that need broad graph traversal.
alternatives.summary: Expose a large all-in-one graph or mega-workspace payload and let clients select slices, derive posture, and coordinate refreshes locally.

criteria.label: Legal truth separation
criteria.rationale: Projection architecture must preserve the separation between authority truth, internal workflow truth, and customer-safe mirrors so no client cache or UI fragment can become legal meaning.

criteria.label: Staff versus customer-safe visibility control
criteria.rationale: The architecture must enforce customer-safe projection boundaries before serialization so portal and customer-visible collaboration routes cannot infer staff-only context.

criteria.label: Same-object and same-shell continuity
criteria.rationale: Rebase, reconnect, notification-open, and responsive collapse must preserve the same object and shell identity where the object still resolves.

criteria.label: Browser/native parity without duplicate business logic
criteria.rationale: Web and native clients must share projection semantics and interaction-layer contracts rather than reimplementing business logic independently.

criteria.label: Stream delta and reconnect fitness
criteria.rationale: The chosen projection strategy must fit monotonic deltas, resume tokens, duplicate idempotency, and typed catch-up rules.

criteria.label: Stale-view protection and rebase clarity
criteria.rationale: Stale-view rejection, rebase, and recovery must remain explicit server-authored contracts so clients do not guess what changed.

criteria.label: Rebuildability from durable truth
criteria.rationale: Streams, caches, and native hydrators must remain disposable and fully rebuildable from durable truth without hidden heuristics.

criteria.label: Schema evolution and reader-window safety
criteria.rationale: Projection contracts need stable versioning and reader-window rules so web, native, and tests can survive additive change while failing closed on incompatible drift.

criteria.label: Performance and cache invalidation complexity
criteria.rationale: Projection design must keep cache invalidation explicit and affordable without pushing semantic composition or truth leakage to the clients.

criteria.label: Testing determinism and fixture friendliness
criteria.rationale: Stable read-model envelopes and selectors make Playwright, API, and native tests deterministic and easier to fixture.

criteria.label: Operational simplicity and debuggability
criteria.rationale: The projection doctrine should be explainable in production incidents without hiding critical semantics inside client code or one giant graph query.
